critique.summary: The article describes 'cliamp,' a terminal-based music player inspired by Winamp that supports playing local files, streams, podcasts, and various online music services with features like a spectrum visualizer and parametric EQ. It is built using several Go libraries and offers multiple installation methods, configuration options, and integration with services like Spotify and YouTube.
mainPoints: cliamp is a retro-style terminal music player inspired by Winamp.
It supports playing local music files, online streams, podcasts, and services such as YouTube, SoundCloud, Spotify, and Navidrome.
Features include a spectrum visualizer, parametric equalizer, playlist management, and keybindings for control.
The software is built with Go and several libraries including Bubbletea, Lip Gloss, Beep, and go-librespot.
Installation options include a shell script, Homebrew, Arch Linux AUR, pre-built binaries, or building from source.
Users can add custom radio stations and host their own radio using cliamp-server.
The project is open source, licensed under MIT, and available on GitHub with active development and multiple releases.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about cliamp are accurate based on the official GitHub repository and documentation. The repository shows active development, multiple releases, and detailed features consistent with the article's description. The supported platforms, features, and installation methods are verifiable through the project's GitHub page and related documentation. There is no indication of misinformation or unsupported claims.
considerations: The article does not mention system requirements or compatibility limitations.
There is no discussion of user experience or performance benchmarks compared to other music players.
Security and privacy implications of streaming from various online services are not addressed.
The article does not provide user reviews or community feedback on the software's reliability or usability.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around a terminal music player inspired by Winamp, with users expressing nostalgia and appreciation for the project. However, there is significant concern and criticism regarding the inclusion of telemetry enabled by default, which some consider unnecessary and intrusive for a local CLI music player.

comments.author: jjshoe
comments.text: Like others the telemetry is hugely undesirable nor necessary. Likewise if they truly don't collect your IP as they claim it's just ripe for endless abuse.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights privacy concerns about telemetry in CLI apps, emphasizing the risk of abuse even if IP collection is denied.

comments.author: Habgdnv
comments.text:
I remember with nostalgia the mp3blaster. I spent years listening to it in my terminal. At one point I used only cli without graphical desktop on slackware and one of my TTYs was dedicated to it.

Turns out these times are forever gone - never to come back. The huge disappointment when I tried this on the first run to play a mp3 file from my local disk and it initiated outbound connection. Why a local CLI player needs outbound TCP connection to play a local file from my local disk?!?! The answer was in the source. It is called telemetry. Back then when I used mp3blaster we used to call this spyware, but the times had changed since then.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a firsthand user experience regarding telemetry, correctly identifying the outbound connection as telemetry and expressing justified disappointment.

comments.author: stutstev
comments.text:
As someone who is fond of Windows music players and futuristically designed DIN stereos of the early-to-mid 2000s, the variety of console visualizations is wicked cool and very much welcome! This is easily the best feature of cliamp. I'd love the collection of visualizations as a separate program, akin to cava[1], that listens and responds to your default audio sink. I already use a Raspberry Pi for music while driving, so I'm already thinking about displaying these visualizations on my car's infotainment screen somehow.

As a friendly request, I'd love to be able to use up and down keys to seek one minute forward or backward during playback, like with mpv. I play a lot of mixes that are an hour or longer in length, so this functionality would be a nice-to-have. I'll likely submit this idea to GitHub, anyhow.

To share some honest criticism, I was disappointed to discover built-in telemetry. Although it can be disabled with a flag, I dislike how it's enabled by default and unknown to the user unless one specifies the -h flag. I don't understand why user diagnostics data is needed from a console music player. Make this anti-feature opt-in and instead rely primarily on bug reports, or make the user aware of this telemetry upon initial invocation and provide instructions on how to disable it. Constructively, know your audience.

But overall, thank you to all the maintainers for this cool software!

[1]: https://github.com/karlstav/cava
comments.analysis: This comment offers a balanced critique, appreciating the software but constructively criticizing the default telemetry setting and suggesting improvements.

comments.author: blamesoft
comments.text: Telemetry on by default in a CLI app seems very weird
comments.analysis: A concise but valid observation that telemetry enabled by default in a CLI app is unusual and potentially unwelcome.

comments.author: Vaslo
comments.text: I’ve been using this in Omarchy, it’s really great - easy to use and can do any songs or playlist on YouTube, so I’ll pipe through those programming concentration playlists without visiting YouTube.
comments.analysis: This comment provides positive user feedback on functionality, highlighting ease of use and YouTube integration.

comments.author: daytonix
comments.text: Just installed this, loving it so far! Thank you!
comments.analysis: A brief positive reaction expressing enjoyment but lacking substantive content.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text:
Winamp was pretty cool. When I switched to Linux, many years ago, I wanted to have winamp too. I think I used bmp for a while until it died; before that xmms but that one also sort of died.

Meanwhile some other GUI showed up, I forgot the name. I kind of gave up on winamp, mostly because my use cases shifted. I went to mplayer, then mpv, and now I am too used to using mpv for literally anything related to audio and video (which in turn uses ffmpeg of course). I kind of built a commandline helper variant that just plays anything I have local - audio, video. I could probably go and find a nice UI again, and that may have advantages such as simply scrolling through the list or setting ad-hoc favourites, but I don't quite need it anymore; I am faster with the keyboard too, so my use cases changed. To play all audio from Hans Zimmer, for instance, I may type "rsong Zimm" or something like that. (I also alias a lot so I may just type "zimmer" instead, but most of the time if I use it I just have it default to random selection as I don't care what is played normally.)
comments.analysis: Provides historical context and personal evolution in music player usage, relevant but less directly related to the current project.

comments.author: poolnoodle
comments.analysis: A humorous but irrelevant comment that adds no meaningful insight.

comments.author: samschooler
comments.text: The project is cool, the demo song is iconically AI not great, sorry.
comments.analysis: A subjective and brief critique of the demo song quality, not contributing to the main discussion.

comments.author: sn0n
comments.text: Imagine telling your AI to include unsecured telemetry… all im the namesake of mother Russia and father China. Wait no, that’s the wrong message.
comments.analysis: This comment is confusing and appears to be a sarcastic or trolling remark with unclear relevance or factual basis.

comments.author: dancnb86
comments.text: Using this opportunity for a shameless plug of my project, TUI radio player: https://github.com/dancnb/sonicradio
comments.analysis: Self-promotion unrelated to the discussion about cliamp's features or issues.

comments.author: flpm
comments.text:
Looks great and bring backs a lot of memories... I had to go back to https://skins.webamp.org/ for a nostalgia shot.

I wish music had not move to stream, I think I appreciated each song much more when listening did not feel like such an ephemeral experience.
comments.analysis: Expresses nostalgia and a personal opinion on music consumption trends, relevant to the theme but not about the software itself.

comments.author: sombragris
comments.text:
For a terminal music player I like termusic, which is somewhat similar to mp3blaster, which I sorely miss.

https://github.com/tramhao/termusic

The not so good parts are 1) it is written in Rust and therefore packaging is awful with a lot of dependencies; 2) it repeats playlists by default with no option (so far) to turn that off. But there's an issue open on it and it looks like this is going to be fixed.
comments.analysis: Offers a comparative perspective on similar software with constructive criticism, though less directly about cliamp.
